meta.originalTitle: The Rise and Fall of Detective Stories 侦探小说的兴衰

children.text: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 1–13, which are based on Reading Passage 1 below.
The Rise and Fall of Detective Stories
Detective stories became hugely popular in the 19th and 20th centuries. William D. Rubinstein looks at why social changes eventually led to their downfall.

The detective story is normally said to have begun in the fertile brain of the great American writer Edgar Allan Poe (1809–49), especially in his stories featuring the detective C. Auguste Dupin. From 1859, Dupin had a counterpart in Monsieur Lecoq, created by the French author Emile Gaboriau. Despite these American and French origins, it was to Britain that detective fiction migrated, where it took root and flourished, becoming a characteristically British genre.

This transition occurred because of one author and his great detective. The most famous of all fictional detectives, Sherlock Holmes, was introduced by Sir Arthur Conan Doyle in A Study in Scarlet, first published in 1887, and later became the subject of four novels and 56 short stories. Nearly all of the Holmes stories are narrated by his friend Dr Watson. Watson is constantly amazed and stupefied by Holmes's genius, but despite years of working with him, Watson is never able to produce these brilliant insights himself. Holmes is memorably eccentric, with a range of endearing and less endearing habits. He is a brilliant private detective, categorically better than the plodding and mediocre officials of Scotland Yard, who constantly turn to him when they are baffled. This in itself is pure fiction: in real life there were never any brilliant private detectives to whom Scotland Yard turned when they failed, and the Yard's Criminal Investigation Department (CID) had a remarkable clear-up rate and was highly competent.

Most of the Holmes stories are set among the higher levels of 19th-century British society, a world inhabited by professional men, retired army officers and country gentlemen, as well as members of royalty and cabinet ministers. Few take place among the working classes or the very poor, whereas in fact much crime was a product of the poverty and gangs in London's underworld.

In the 20th century detective stories became increasingly popular. Reading these stories was one of the characteristic aspects of the British middle classes in the 'golden age' of British detective fiction—the 1920s and 30s. Their emphasis on rationality, the inevitable triumph of justice, and the existence of an unofficial super-detective tells us much about the society of the time. So, too, do the stock characters and unstated prejudices in these works: country folk and domestic servants were almost always depicted as unintelligent, and women were often depicted in a simplistic, two-dimensional way, although a few female detective writers would present female characters in a more realistic manner.

Most of the well-known British authors of ‘golden age' detective stories were drawn from the middle classes, like their audience. Conan Doyle was a doctor who turned to writing fiction while he awaited his patients; Freeman Wills Crofts was a railway engineer in Northern Ireland; Gilbert Keith Chesterton and Anthony Berkeley were journalists; Cecil Street a career army officer. Apart from a few superstars such as Agatha Christie, financial rewards for these interwar authors were rather meagre; a few hundred pounds per book—a useful income, but nothing princely.

US writers such as Rex Stout and Ellery Queen attempted to recreate the 'golden age' of British detective fiction. For the most part their books were mere imitations of the British models, although they were seldom wholly successful. But in the 1920s and 1930s, America also saw the rise of the 'hard-boiled' genre and its detective type: the tough private cop who appeared in the works of Dashiell Hammett and Raymond Chandler. Apart from the violence that appeared throughout their works, Hammett's and Chandler's novels were often marked by a political agenda that sought to expose the inequality they saw at the heart of American life. Britain had no real parallel either to their outlook on the world or (until much later) to their violence, but upheld the belief that the authorities should punish criminals regardless of their circumstances.

By around 1960, the classic British detective story was in serious decline. It seemed that writers had simply run out of ingenious plots and puzzles for their detectives to solve. The best-known crime fiction writers, such as P. D. James, eschewed private detectives for police inspectors, and straightforward puzzles for stories that were full of unexpected twists.

Today the detective story no longer exists in Britain, at least in its old form. Arguably this mirrors the transformation of that society as a whole. The belief that scientific developments were invariably beneficial possibly reached its height during the period when the classic detective story flourished, as did the belief in putting rationality at the heart of Britain's education system. And finally, the central belief that evil-doers would inevitably get their just deserts through the incorruptibility of the judicial system was replaced by a questioning of some of the procedures and decisions associated with that system.
Do the following statements agree with the information given in Reading Passage 1?
In boxes 1–8 on your answer sheet, write:
if the statement agrees with the information
if the statement contradicts the information
if there is no information on this
C. Auguste Dupin and Émile Gaboriau were both writers of detective stories.
It was Conan Doyle's creation of Sherlock Holmes that made the detective story a typically British genre.
The positive qualities of the character of Sherlock Holmes outweigh the negative qualities.
Officials at Scotland Yard were unhappy at the way they were portrayed in the Sherlock Holmes stories.
Sherlock Holmes is based on a real private detective who was consulted by Scotland Yard.
Conan Doyle's work fails to reflect the reality of crime in 19th-century Britain.
In the 1920s and 1930s, most writers of detective stories started to include interesting female characters in their work.
Agatha Christie only earned a few hundred pounds for her books.

explanationSection.text:
题目翻译：奥古斯特・杜宾与埃米尔・加博里约均为侦探小说作家。
答案：FALSE（错误）
解析：定位 Paragraph 1 中 “in his stories featuring the detective C. Auguste Dupin... Dupin had a counterpart in Monsieur Lecoq, created by the French author Emile Gaboriau”，明确提到 “杜宾是侦探角色”，加博里约才是作家，与题干 “两人均为作家” 描述矛盾，因此判定为 FALSE。
题目翻译：柯南・道尔创造的夏洛克・福尔摩斯，使侦探小说成为英国特色文学类型。
答案：TRUE（正确）
解析：定位 Paragraph 1 中 “despite these American and French origins, it was to Britain that detective fiction migrated, where it took root and flourished, becoming a characteristically British genre. This transition occurred because of one author and his great detective. The most famous of all fictional detectives, Sherlock Holmes, was introduced by Sir Arthur Conan Doyle”，明确提到侦探小说转变为英国特色类型，归因于柯南・道尔创造的福尔摩斯，与题干描述一致，因此判定为 TRUE。
题目翻译：夏洛克・福尔摩斯角色的优点多于缺点。
答案：NOT GIVEN（未提及）
解析：定位 Paragraph 2 中 “Holmes is memorably eccentric, with a range of endearing and less endearing habits. He is a brilliant private detective”，仅罗列福尔摩斯的 “可爱与不可爱之处”，未比较 “优点是否多于缺点”，无相关信息，因此判定为 NOT GIVEN。
题目翻译：苏格兰场官员对福尔摩斯故事中自身的形象塑造感到不满。
答案：NOT GIVEN（未提及）
解析：定位 Paragraph 2 中 “the plodding and mediocre officials of Scotland Yard... This in itself is pure fiction: in real life... the Yard’s Criminal Investigation Department (CID) had a remarkable clear-up rate and was highly competent”，仅说明故事与现实中苏格兰场官员的差异，未提及官员 “是否不满”，无相关信息，因此判定为 NOT GIVEN。
题目翻译：夏洛克・福尔摩斯以一位被苏格兰场咨询的真实私家侦探为原型。
答案：FALSE（错误）
解析：定位 Paragraph 2 中 “This in itself is pure fiction: in real life there were never any brilliant private detectives to whom Scotland Yard turned when they failed”，明确提到现实中 “不存在苏格兰场求助的私家侦探”，与题干 “以真实侦探为原型” 描述矛盾，因此判定为 FALSE。
题目翻译：柯南・道尔的作品未反映 19 世纪英国犯罪的现实。
答案：TRUE（正确）
解析：定位 Paragraph 3 中 “Few take place among the working classes or the very poor, whereas in fact much crime was a product of the poverty and gangs in London’s underworld”，明确提到作品多写上层社会，未反映 “犯罪源于贫困与帮派” 的现实，与题干描述一致，因此判定为 TRUE。
题目翻译：20 世纪 20-30 年代，多数侦探小说作家开始在作品中加入有趣的女性角色。
答案：FALSE（错误）
解析：定位 Paragraph 4 中 “women were often depicted in a simplistic, two-dimensional way, although a few female detective writers would present female characters in a more realistic manner”，明确提到 “多数作家将女性写得扁平”，仅 “少数女作家” 塑造真实女性角色，与题干 “多数作家加入有趣女性角色” 描述矛盾，因此判定为 FALSE。
题目翻译：阿加莎・克里斯蒂的书籍仅赚了几百英镑。
答案：FALSE（错误）
解析：定位 Paragraph 5 中 “Apart from a few superstars such as Agatha Christie, financial rewards for these interwar authors were rather meagre; a few hundred pounds per book”，明确提到 “几百英镑 / 本是多数作家的收入”，阿加莎属于 “少数巨星”，收入远超此数，与题干描述矛盾，因此判定为 FALSE。

items.text:
（1）题目 1：C. Auguste Dupin and Émile Gaboriau were both writers of detective stories.
题目翻译：奥古斯特・杜宾与埃米尔・加博里约均为侦探小说作家。
答案：FALSE（错误）
解析：定位 Paragraph 1 中 “in his stories featuring the detective C. Auguste Dupin... Dupin had a counterpart in Monsieur Lecoq, created by the French author Emile Gaboriau”，明确提到 “杜宾是侦探角色”，加博里约才是作家，与题干 “两人均为作家” 描述矛盾，因此判定为 FALSE。

（2）题目 2：It was Conan Doyle’s creation of Sherlock Holmes that made the detective story a typically British genre.
题目翻译：柯南・道尔创造的夏洛克・福尔摩斯，使侦探小说成为英国特色文学类型。
答案：TRUE（正确）
解析：定位 Paragraph 1 中 “despite these American and French origins, it was to Britain that detective fiction migrated, where it took root and flourished, becoming a characteristically British genre. This transition occurred because of one author and his great detective. The most famous of all fictional detectives, Sherlock Holmes, was introduced by Sir Arthur Conan Doyle”，明确提到侦探小说转变为英国特色类型，归因于柯南・道尔创造的福尔摩斯，与题干描述一致，因此判定为 TRUE。

（3）题目 3：The positive qualities of the character of Sherlock Holmes outweigh the negative qualities.
题目翻译：夏洛克・福尔摩斯角色的优点多于缺点。
答案：NOT GIVEN（未提及）
解析：定位 Paragraph 2 中 “Holmes is memorably eccentric, with a range of endearing and less endearing habits. He is a brilliant private detective”，仅罗列福尔摩斯的 “可爱与不可爱之处”，未比较 “优点是否多于缺点”，无相关信息，因此判定为 NOT GIVEN。

（4）题目 4：Officials at Scotland Yard were unhappy at the way they were portrayed in the Sherlock Holmes stories.
题目翻译：苏格兰场官员对福尔摩斯故事中自身的形象塑造感到不满。
答案：NOT GIVEN（未提及）
解析：定位 Paragraph 2 中 “the plodding and mediocre officials of Scotland Yard... This in itself is pure fiction: in real life... the Yard’s Criminal Investigation Department (CID) had a remarkable clear-up rate and was highly competent”，仅说明故事与现实中苏格兰场官员的差异，未提及官员 “是否不满”，无相关信息，因此判定为 NOT GIVEN。

（5）题目 5：Sherlock Holmes is based on a real private detective who was consulted by Scotland Yard.
题目翻译：夏洛克・福尔摩斯以一位被苏格兰场咨询的真实私家侦探为原型。
答案：FALSE（错误）
解析：定位 Paragraph 2 中 “This in itself is pure fiction: in real life there were never any brilliant private detectives to whom Scotland Yard turned when they failed”，明确提到现实中 “不存在苏格兰场求助的私家侦探”，与题干 “以真实侦探为原型” 描述矛盾，因此判定为 FALSE。

（6）题目 6：Conan Doyle’s work fails to reflect the reality of crime in 19th-century Britain.
题目翻译：柯南・道尔的作品未反映 19 世纪英国犯罪的现实。
答案：TRUE（正确）
解析：定位 Paragraph 3 中 “Few take place among the working classes or the very poor, whereas in fact much crime was a product of the poverty and gangs in London’s underworld”，明确提到作品多写上层社会，未反映 “犯罪源于贫困与帮派” 的现实，与题干描述一致，因此判定为 TRUE。

（7）题目 7：In the 1920s and 1930s, most writers of detective stories started to include interesting female characters in their work.
题目翻译：20 世纪 20-30 年代，多数侦探小说作家开始在作品中加入有趣的女性角色。
答案：FALSE（错误）
解析：定位 Paragraph 4 中 “women were often depicted in a simplistic, two-dimensional way, although a few female detective writers would present female characters in a more realistic manner”，明确提到 “多数作家将女性写得扁平”，仅 “少数女作家” 塑造真实女性角色，与题干 “多数作家加入有趣女性角色” 描述矛盾，因此判定为 FALSE。

（8）题目 8：Agatha Christie only earned a few hundred pounds for her books.
题目翻译：阿加莎・克里斯蒂的书籍仅赚了几百英镑。
答案：FALSE（错误）
解析：定位 Paragraph 5 中 “Apart from a few superstars such as Agatha Christie, financial rewards for these interwar authors were rather meagre; a few hundred pounds per book”，明确提到 “几百英镑 / 本是多数作家的收入”，阿加莎属于 “少数巨星”，收入远超此数，与题干描述矛盾，因此判定为 FALSE。

children.text: from the passage for each answer.
Rex Stout and Ellery Queen's works:
message about the unfairness of society
Writers such as P. D. James:
wrote about police inspectors, not private detectives
The end of the traditional detective story reflects social changes. For example:
scientific progress is not always seen as
less trust is now placed in rationality
there is more questioning of the judicial system

explanationSection.text:
题目翻译：美国 20-30 年代 - 雷克斯・斯托特与埃勒里・奎因的作品 - 主要是不成功的英国侦探小说_\___\__
答案：imitations（模仿品）
解析：定位 Paragraph 6 中 “US writers such as Rex Stout and Ellery Queen attempted to recreate the ‘golden age’ of British detective fiction. For the most part their books were mere imitations of the British models, although they were seldom wholly successful”，“mere imitations” 对应 “unsuccessful \___\___”，因此答案为 “imitations”。
题目翻译：美国 20-30 年代 - 哈米特与钱德勒的作品 - 充斥着_\___\__
答案：violence（暴力）
解析：定位 Paragraph 6 中 “Apart from the violence that appeared throughout their works, Hammett’s and Chandler’s novels were often marked by a political agenda”，明确提到两人作品 “遍布暴力”，因此答案为 “violence”。
题目翻译：美国 20-30 年代 - 哈米特与钱德勒的作品 - 带有关于社会不公的_\___\__主张
答案：political（政治的）
解析：定位 Paragraph 6 中 “their novels were often marked by a political agenda that sought to expose the inequality they saw at the heart of American life”，“political agenda” 对应 “\___\___ message”，因此答案为 “political”。
题目翻译：英国 50-60 年代 - P.D. 詹姆斯等作家 - 创作充满_\___\__的故事
答案：twists（转折）
解析：定位 Paragraph 7 中 “the best-known crime fiction writers, such as P. D. James... and straightforward puzzles for stories that were full of unexpected twists”，“full of unexpected twists” 对应 “with many \___\___”，因此答案为 “twists”。
题目翻译：英国 如今 - 社会变革 - 科技进步不再总被视为_\___\__
答案：beneficial（有益的）
解析：定位 Paragraph 8 中 “The belief that scientific developments were invariably beneficial possibly reached its height during the period when the classic detective story flourished”，如今社会变革意味着科技进步 “并非总被视为有益”，因此答案为 “beneficial”。

items.text:
（1）题目 9：USA 1920s–1930s - Rex Stout and Ellery Queen’s works - were mainly unsuccessful \___\___ of British detective fiction
题目翻译：美国 20-30 年代 - 雷克斯・斯托特与埃勒里・奎因的作品 - 主要是不成功的英国侦探小说_\___\__
答案：imitations（模仿品）
解析：定位 Paragraph 6 中 “US writers such as Rex Stout and Ellery Queen attempted to recreate the ‘golden age’ of British detective fiction. For the most part their books were mere imitations of the British models, although they were seldom wholly successful”，“mere imitations” 对应 “unsuccessful \___\___”，因此答案为 “imitations”。

（2）题目 10：USA 1920s–1930s - Hammett and Chandler’s works - were full of \___\___
题目翻译：美国 20-30 年代 - 哈米特与钱德勒的作品 - 充斥着_\___\__
答案：violence（暴力）
解析：定位 Paragraph 6 中 “Apart from the violence that appeared throughout their works, Hammett’s and Chandler’s novels were often marked by a political agenda”，明确提到两人作品 “遍布暴力”，因此答案为 “violence”。

（3）题目 11：USA 1920s–1930s - Hammett and Chandler’s works - had a \___\___ message about the unfairness of society
题目翻译：美国 20-30 年代 - 哈米特与钱德勒的作品 - 带有关于社会不公的_\___\__主张
答案：political（政治的）
解析：定位 Paragraph 6 中 “their novels were often marked by a political agenda that sought to expose the inequality they saw at the heart of American life”，“political agenda” 对应 “\___\___ message”，因此答案为 “political”。

（4）题目 12：Britain 1950s–1960s - Writers such as P. D. James - created stories with many \___\___
题目翻译：英国 50-60 年代 - P.D. 詹姆斯等作家 - 创作充满_\___\__的故事
答案：twists（转折）
解析：定位 Paragraph 7 中 “the best-known crime fiction writers, such as P. D. James... and straightforward puzzles for stories that were full of unexpected twists”，“full of unexpected twists” 对应 “with many \___\___”，因此答案为 “twists”。

（5）题目 13：Britain Present day - social changes - scientific progress is not always seen as \___\___
题目翻译：英国 如今 - 社会变革 - 科技进步不再总被视为_\___\__
答案：beneficial（有益的）
解析：定位 Paragraph 8 中 “The belief that scientific developments were invariably beneficial possibly reached its height during the period when the classic detective story flourished”，如今社会变革意味着科技进步 “并非总被视为有益”，因此答案为 “beneficial”。
